claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to understand a bit about your situation.

**First, let me ask about your child:**

Do you have a child (or children) you're claiming for? If so, could you tell me:
1. Their name(s)
2. Their age(s)

This will help me determine which eligibility pathway applies to your situation. Child Benefit eligibility depends significantly on the child's age:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over don't qualify

What is your child's age?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! So you have Alex, who is 8 years old and lives with you at home.

Since Alex is under 16, let me continue with the assessment. The next question is important:

**Is Alex working in full-time paid employment?**

By "full-time," this typically means working 24 hours or more per week. This is quite rare for an 8-year-old, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not working at all. He's just 8, so he's still in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense! Since Alex is 8 years old, in primary school, and not working, let me move to the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now, the next question:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?**

For example, benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed in his own name (not yours). 

Given that Alex is only 8 years old, this is very unlikely, but I need to confirm for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's only 8, so everything goes through me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's correct - makes perfect sense for an 8-year-old! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now the next question:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program?**

Again, since Alex is only 8 years old and in primary school, he wouldn't be in an apprenticeship, but I need to confirm this for completeness.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not. He's only 8 and in primary school, so no apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask about your residency status, as this is a key requirement for Child Benefit:

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

If you live in the UK on a regular basis, that satisfies the residency requirement. 

Alternatively, you can also qualify if you meet other special circumstances such as:
- Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (with certain conditions)
- Being a Crown servant living abroad
- Living in certain qualifying countries

Do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirement. Let me move to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex:

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You mentioned earlier that Alex lives at home with you, which would satisfy this requirement. Responsibility means either:
- Living with the child, OR
- Contributing to their upkeep (paying at least the amount of Child Benefit towards their care - things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)

Since Alex lives with you at home, you meet this requirement, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Alex lives with me at home, so I'm responsible for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask about other potential claimants:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member or guardian
- Anyone else who might be claiming

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at the same time. If someone else is claiming, they would need to agree to transfer the claim to you, or HMRC would need to decide who should claim.

Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, actually. My ex-partner already claims Child Benefit for Alex, and Alex lives with them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see - thank you for clarifying. This is an important detail. You mentioned earlier that Alex lives at home with you, but now you're saying Alex lives with your ex-partner who is claiming Child Benefit. Let me make sure I understand correctly:

**Where does Alex primarily live?**
- Does Alex live with you?
- Does Alex live with your ex-partner?
- Or does Alex split time between both homes?

And regarding the Child Benefit claim:

**Is there any agreement between you and your ex-partner about who should be claiming Child Benefit?**
- Are you trying to transfer the claim from your ex-partner to you (with their agreement)?
- Or is there a dispute about who should be claiming?

This matters because:
- Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at a time
- If you both agree on who should claim, the claim can be transferred
- If there's a dispute and you both want to claim, HMRC would need to make the decision based on who has primary responsibility

Could you clarify Alex's living situation and whether you and your ex-partner agree on who should claim?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Sorry, let me be clear - Alex lives with my ex-partner, and my ex-partner is the one claiming Child Benefit for him. I was asking about whether I might be eligible to claim, but if Alex lives with them and they're already claiming, I guess that affects things.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying! Yes, this is a crucial detail. Let me explain how this affects your eligibility:

**The situation is:**
- Alex lives with your ex-partner
- Your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at a time

**Here are your options:**

1. **If you and your ex-partner agree that you should claim instead** (perhaps because circumstances have changed or Alex will be moving to live with you), then your ex-partner would need to stop their claim, and you could then apply. This is called a transfer of claim.

2. **If there's a dispute** - if you both want to claim and can't agree who should claim - then HMRC would need to decide based on who has primary responsibility for Alex (mainly who Alex lives with most of the time).

3. **If Alex lives with your ex-partner and they continue to claim** - then you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, as they are already the claimant and Alex lives with them.

**My questions to determine your path forward:**

- **Is there an agreement between you and your ex-partner to transfer the claim to you?** 
- **Or are you and your ex-partner in dispute about who should claim?**
- **Or is your ex-partner continuing to claim and Alex will continue living with them?**

Which of these situations applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: My ex-partner is continuing to claim and Alex lives with them. I was just curious about whether I could claim, but it sounds like I can't since they're already claiming and Alex lives with them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
You're absolutely right in your understanding. Since your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex and Alex lives with them, **you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

Here's why:
- **Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child** - your ex-partner is already the claimant
- **The person claiming should be the one with primary responsibility** - since Alex lives with your ex-partner, they are the appropriate claimant
- There's no dispute or agreement to transfer the claim

Let me finalize this assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Actually, let me reconsider the assessment based on your actual situation. Since your ex-partner is already claiming and Alex lives with them, I need to provide you with the correct outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Assessment Complete: You Are Not Eligible**

**Result for Alex:** Not eligible for you to claim

**Reason:** 
Child Benefit is already being claimed for Alex by your ex-partner, and Alex lives with them. Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time. The appropriate claimant is the person with primary responsibility for the child - typically the person the child lives with.

---

### **What This Means:**

✗ You cannot make a new claim for Child Benefit for Alex while your ex-partner is claiming and Alex lives with them

---

### **Your Options If Circumstances Change:**

**If Alex moves to live with you:** You could then ask your ex-partner to end their claim, and you could apply for Child Benefit yourself.

**If you and your ex-partner agree you should claim instead:** Your ex-partner would need to stop their claim first, then you could apply. This might make sense if circumstances have changed regarding who has primary responsibility.

**If you disagree about who should claim:** You could contact HMRC, and they would make a decision based on who has primary responsibility for Alex (considering factors like who Alex lives with most of the time).

---

### **Useful Information:**

📞 **Child Benefit Helpline:** 0300 200 3100  
🌐 **More information:** [www.gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit)

If you have any questions about your specific situation or if circumstances change in the future, please don't hesitate to reach out!

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and would normally qualify as a child under 16. However, Child Benefit is already being claimed for Alex by the claimant's ex-partner, and Alex lives with the ex-partner. Under Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time. The person who claims should be the one with primary responsibility for the child, which is typically the person the child lives with. Since Alex lives with the ex-partner and they are continuing to claim Child Benefit, the claimant is not eligible to make a competing claim for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for their son Alex because Alex's other parent (the claimant's ex-partner) is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and Alex lives with that parent. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at a time, and the appropriate claimant is the one with primary responsibility - in this case, the parent Alex lives with.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old and would normally qualify as a child under 16. However, Child Benefit is already being claimed for Alex by the claimant's ex-partner, and Alex lives with the ex-partner. Under Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time. The person who claims should be the one with primary responsibility for the child, which is typically the person the child lives with. Since Alex lives with the ex-partner and they are continuing to claim Child Benefit, the claimant is not eligible to make a competing claim for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for their son Alex because Alex's other parent (the claimant's ex-partner) is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and Alex lives with that parent. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at a time, and the appropriate claimant is the one with primary responsibility - in this case, the parent Alex lives with.
